notes.section_title: Introduction: Understanding Motion and Its Causes

content.text: For centuries, the nature of motion and its causes remained a mystery. Observations from everyday life suggested that rest was the 'natural state' of objects. However, Galileo Galilei and Isaac Newton revolutionized our understanding of motion by developing a comprehensive approach to study forces and their effects.

A force cannot be directly seen, tasted, or felt. However, we always observe or feel the effects of a force. A force can be understood through its effects: pushing, pulling, or hitting objects changes their state of motion. Force is the cause that produces motion or changes the motion of objects.

content.title: Definition of Force
content.text: Force is a push, pull, or hit applied to an object that can change its state of motion, direction, or shape. It is the agent responsible for accelerating objects or changing their direction of motion.

content.title: Effects of Force
items: Change the magnitude of velocity (make objects move faster or slower)
Change the shape and size of objects (deformation)

content.text: When two or more forces of equal magnitude act on an object in opposite directions, they are called balanced forces. Balanced forces do not change the state of rest or motion of an object. For example, if a wooden block is pulled from both sides with equal forces (equal strings pulling left and right with the same magnitude), the block will not move.
The key characteristic of balanced forces is that they cancel each other out, resulting in zero net force. The net force (or resultant force) is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object.

content.title: Balanced Forces
content.text: Forces are said to be balanced when their resultant (vector sum) is zero. Balanced forces do not produce acceleration in an object — it either remains at rest or continues moving with uniform velocity.

When two opposite forces of different magnitudes act on an object, they are unbalanced forces. The object will move in the direction of the larger force. The difference between the two forces is called the net unbalanced force or resultant force, which produces acceleration.

content.title: Example: Pushing a Box on a Rough Floor
content.description: Consider children trying to push a box on a rough floor. Friction acts opposite to the direction of pushing. (a) If pushing force is small, friction balances it — box does not move (balanced forces). (b) If pushing force is increased but still less than friction, the box still doesn't move (balanced forces). (c) If pushing force exceeds friction, the box accelerates forward (unbalanced forces producing net force in the direction of push).

content.title: Unbalanced Forces
content.text: When the net force acting on an object is not zero, the forces are unbalanced. An unbalanced force always produces acceleration in the direction of the net force. The greater the unbalanced force, the greater the acceleration produced.

An important observation: to keep an object moving at constant velocity, the unbalanced force does NOT need to be continuously applied. In the absence of friction or other resistive forces, an object moving with uniform velocity will continue to do so without any net external force. However, to overcome friction and maintain motion, a continuous force must be applied — but this force only needs to equal the friction force (keeping forces balanced) to maintain constant velocity.

notes.section_title: 8.2 First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)

topics.topic_name: Galileo's Observations and Development of the First Law

content.text: Galileo Galilei conducted a series of experiments using inclined planes to understand motion. He observed that when a marble rolls down an inclined plane, its velocity increases due to gravity. When it rolls up an inclined plane, its velocity decreases due to the opposing effect of gravity.

Galileo then imagined an ideal scenario with a marble resting on a double inclined plane (slopes on both sides). If the marble is released from a height on the left side, it rolls down and up the right side, reaching nearly the same height (with minimal loss due to friction). As the inclination of the right-side slope is gradually decreased (made flatter), the marble would travel farther to reach the original height. If the right side becomes horizontal (zero inclination), the marble would theoretically travel forever trying to reach the original height.

content.title: Key Insight from Galileo's Experiment
content.text: In the absence of unbalanced forces (no friction, no gravity component), an object in motion continues to move indefinitely with constant velocity. This suggests that an object does not need a continuous force to maintain uniform motion — only to change its state of motion.

In practical situations, we always observe friction, which acts opposite to motion. This is why objects eventually stop moving. However, minimizing friction (using smooth surfaces and lubricants) shows that objects can move much farther without external driving forces.

content.title: Newton's First Law of Motion
content.text: An object remains in a state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled to change that state by an applied unbalanced force.

This law is also known as the Law of Inertia. It states two things: (1) Objects naturally resist changes in their motion, and (2) no net external force is required to maintain uniform motion — only to initiate or stop motion.

content.meaning: The natural tendency of an object to resist a change in its state of motion or rest. It is a qualitative measure of how much an object resists acceleration. All objects possess inertia — it is a fundamental property of matter.

topics.topic_name: Everyday Examples of the First Law (Inertia)

content.title: Examples from Daily Life
items: Sudden braking of a car: Passengers tend to move forward due to inertia (body continues in original state of motion even though car decelerates). Safety belts apply a force to reduce forward motion.
Sudden acceleration of a bus: Standing passengers tend to fall backward because the bus accelerates forward, but the passenger's body tends to remain at rest due to inertia.

Sharp turn at high speed: Passengers are thrown to one side due to inertia (body tends to continue in straight-line motion while car changes direction). This is why a centripetal force is required for circular motion.

Removing a cloth from beneath dishes: If done with a sharp, quick pull, dishes remain in place due to inertia (minimal contact time, minimal force applied to dishes). If pulled slowly, friction acts longer and dishes move with the cloth.

content.title: Activities Demonstrating Inertia
content.text: Activity 8.1 (Carom coin stack): A fast-moving coin strikes the bottom of a stack. Due to the brief contact, the bottom coin quickly moves out, while other coins fall vertically due to inertia. Activity 8.2 (Coin on card): A card is flicked from under a coin resting on top. The brief force acts on the card, not the coin. By inertia, the coin remains at rest and falls into the glass below. Activity 8.3 (Water in spinning tray): When a tray with water is spun rapidly, water tends to maintain straight-line motion due to inertia and spills outward.

All objects resist changes in their state of motion — this resistance is called inertia. However, the degree of inertia varies from object to object. Some objects resist change more than others. For example, it is easier to push an empty box than a box full of books. A football easily flies away when kicked, but a stone of similar size barely moves and might injure your foot. A force sufficient to accelerate a small cart to high velocity produces negligible change in a train's motion.

The difference in resistance to motion is due to differences in mass. Heavier or more massive objects offer greater resistance to acceleration — they have more inertia. Quantitatively, inertia is measured by an object's mass.

content.meaning: A measure of the amount of matter in an object or a measure of the object's inertia. Mass is an intrinsic property of matter and does not change with location. It is a scalar quantity measured in kilograms (kg) in the SI system.

content.title: Important Distinction: Mass vs Weight
content.text: Mass and weight are different. Mass is the amount of matter and remains constant everywhere. Weight is the gravitational force on an object and depends on location (W = mg, where g varies with location). An object has the same mass on Earth and Moon, but different weights.
In this chapter, we focus on mass as the measure of inertia. The greater an object's mass, the greater its inertia and the greater the force required to produce a given acceleration.

content.title: Examples Comparing Inertia
content.description: Which has more inertia? (a) A rubber ball and a stone of same size? Answer: The stone has more inertia (greater mass). (b) A bicycle and a train? Answer: The train has vastly more inertia (much greater mass). (c) A five-rupee coin and one-rupee coin? Answer: The five-rupee coin has more inertia (slightly greater mass). In all cases, the object with greater mass has greater inertia.

content.text: Understanding inertia and mass is crucial for applying Newton's laws. As mass increases, for a given applied force, the acceleration decreases (F = ma, so a = F/m). This is the essence of the second law of motion.

topics.topic_name: Motivation: Why Do We Need the Second Law?

content.text: The first law tells us what happens when no unbalanced force acts on an object. The second law answers the question: when an unbalanced force does act, what is the relationship between the force, mass, and resulting acceleration? In other words, how do we measure and quantify force?

From everyday observations: (1) A table tennis ball hitting a spectator doesn't hurt, but a cricket ball at the same speed causes injury. (2) A truck parked on a roadside is harmless, but moving at even 5 m/s can be fatal. (3) A bullet can penetrate objects, while a ball of the same mass moving slowly cannot. These observations suggest that both mass and velocity of an object matter in determining the impact it produces.

content.meaning: The product of an object's mass and velocity. It is a vector quantity with the same direction as velocity. Momentum represents the 'quantity of motion' an object possesses.

content.text: Momentum is mathematically defined as: p = mv, where p is momentum (kg·m/s), m is mass (kg), and v is velocity (m/s). An object with greater mass or greater velocity has greater momentum. Two objects with the same kinetic energy can have different momenta, and vice versa.

content.title: Momentum Units
content.text: The SI unit of momentum is kilogram-metre per second (kg·m/s). A single unit of momentum represents the momentum of a 1 kg object moving at 1 m/s.

content.title: Comparing Momenta
content.description: A 1000 kg truck moving at 5 m/s has momentum p = 1000 × 5 = 5000 kg·m/s. A 1500 kg car moving at 10 m/s has momentum p = 1500 × 10 = 15000 kg·m/s. Even though both are moving, the car has three times more momentum, reflecting its greater 'quantity of motion' and potentially greater impact.

topics.topic_name: Force and Rate of Change of Momentum

content.text: When an unbalanced force is applied to an object, its velocity changes. Since momentum depends on velocity, a change in velocity means a change in momentum. The critical insight: the force applied determines how quickly momentum changes.

Consider pushing a car with a dead battery to start the engine. A sudden, brief push (large force for short time) may not be enough. A continuous push (moderate force for longer time) gradually accelerates the car to the required speed. This shows that the change in momentum depends on both the force magnitude and the time duration for which it is applied.

content.title: Newton's Second Law of Motion (Qualitative)
content.text: The rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of the force. In other words, Force ∝ (Change in momentum / Time). The SI unit of force is defined such that the proportionality constant equals 1.
Let an object of mass m have initial velocity u and be uniformly accelerated to velocity v in time t by a constant force F. The initial momentum is p₁ = mu and final momentum is p₂ = mv.
Change in momentum = p₂ - p₁ = mv - mu = m(v - u)
Rate of change of momentum = m(v - u) / t
According to Newton's second law: F ∝ m(v - u) / t, which can be written as F = k·m(v - u) / t, where k is a constant of proportionality.
Since (v - u) / t = a (acceleration), we have: F = k·m·a

content.title: Definition of Newton (Unit of Force)
content.text: The SI unit of force is chosen such that k = 1. One newton (N) is defined as the force that produces an acceleration of 1 m/s² in a 1 kg object. Therefore: 1 N = 1 kg × 1 m/s² = 1 kg·m/s²
This gives us the fundamental equation of the second law: F = m·a, where F is force in newtons (N), m is mass in kilograms (kg), and a is acceleration in meters per second squared (m/s²).

topics.topic_name: Alternative Forms of the Second Law

content.text: From F = m·a and the definition of acceleration a = (v - u) / t, we can derive an alternative form:
F = m(v - u) / t, which rearranges to: F·t = m(v - u) = m·v - m·u = p₂ - p₁ = Δp

content.title: Impulse-Momentum Form
content.text: F·t = Δp, where F·t is called the impulse (force applied over time) and Δp is the change in momentum. This form emphasizes that the change in momentum equals the impulse applied. A large force for short time can produce the same momentum change as a small force for long time if their product (impulse) is equal.

topics.topic_name: Deriving the First Law from the Second Law

content.text: From the second law: F = m·a or F = m(v - u) / t, we get: F·t = m(v - u)
When F = 0 (no unbalanced force), we have: 0 = m(v - u), which gives v = u for any time t.

content.title: Derivation of First Law
content.text: This means that if no unbalanced force acts (F = 0), the velocity remains constant (v = u). If the object was initially at rest (u = 0), it remains at rest. If it was initially in motion (u ≠ 0), it continues with that uniform velocity. This is exactly the first law of motion — derived from the second law with F = 0. The first law is thus a special case of the second law.

topics.topic_name: Practical Applications of the Second Law

content.title: Real-World Examples
items: Catching a fast cricket ball: A fielder pulls hands backward with the moving ball, increasing the time over which the ball's velocity decreases to zero. By Impulse-Momentum form (F·t = Δp), increasing time t decreases the required force F, reducing injury risk.
High jump landing: Athletes land on cushioned or sand beds to increase the time taken to stop. Greater time means lower deceleration, hence lower force on the body.
Karate board breaking: The performer applies a large force (impact force from hand) for a very short time, delivering large impulse (F·t) that causes momentum change in the board, breaking it.
Airbags in cars: During collision, airbags deploy and cushion the passenger. They increase the time over which the passenger's momentum is reduced, decreasing the force experienced.
Rocket propulsion: By expelling gases at high velocity (changing the rocket's momentum), large forces are generated to accelerate the rocket.

notes.section_title: 8.4.1 Solved Examples on Second Law of Motion

content.title: Example 8.1: Force and Acceleration
content.description: A constant force acts on an object of mass 5 kg for a duration of 2 s. It increases the object's velocity from 3 m/s to 7 m/s. Find the magnitude of the applied force. If the force was applied for 5 s instead, what would be the final velocity?

content.text: Solution: Given: m = 5 kg, u = 3 m/s, v = 7 m/s, t = 2 s. Using F = m(v - u) / t:
For t = 5 s with same force F = 10 N, using v = u + (F·t / m):

content.title: Example 8.2: Comparing Forces
content.description: Which requires greater force: accelerating a 2 kg mass at 5 m/s² or a 4 kg mass at 2 m/s²?

content.text: Solution: Using F = m·a:
For case 1: F₁ = 2 kg × 5 m/s² = 10 N
For case 2: F₂ = 4 kg × 2 m/s² = 8 N

Since F₁ (10 N) > F₂ (8 N), accelerating the 2 kg mass at 5 m/s² requires greater force. This shows that force depends on both mass and acceleration — doubling mass while halving acceleration results in a net decrease in force.

content.title: Example 8.3: Braking Force on a Vehicle
content.description: A motorcar moving at 108 km/h takes 4 s to stop after brakes are applied. The total mass (car + passengers) is 1000 kg. Calculate the force exerted by the brakes.

content.text: Solution: Convert u = 108 km/h = 108 × (1000 / 3600) = 30 m/s. Final velocity v = 0 m/s, t = 4 s, m = 1000 kg.
Using F = m(v - u) / t:

The negative sign indicates the force is opposite to the direction of motion (braking force). Magnitude = 7500 N. This large force is why careful braking is essential and why safety features like airbags are important.

content.title: Newton's Third Law of Motion
content.text: To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. When one object exerts a force on another object, the second object instantaneously exerts a force back on the first object. These two forces are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction.

A key point: action and reaction forces act on different objects, never on the same object. In a football collision, the player exerts a force on the ball (action), and the ball exerts an equal and opposite force on the player (reaction). Both forces are real and instantaneous.

Action and reaction can be identified by asking: 'Which object exerts the force?' and 'On which object is it exerted?' If object A exerts force on object B, then B exerts reaction force back on A. Neither is more 'primary' — they always exist together as a pair.

content.title: Critical Understanding
content.text: Action and reaction forces always act on different objects. They do NOT cancel each other out. If they acted on the same object, they would cancel and no motion would occur. But since they act on different objects, each can produce acceleration in its respective object. The accelerations may differ based on the masses of the objects.

Two spring balances can be connected in a line, with one end fixed to a rigid wall and the other end free to be pulled. When force is applied through the free end, both spring balances show identical readings on their scales.

Explanation: The force applied through the free balance (say, F = 5 N pulling right) is transmitted through the system. The fixed balance records this force as 5 N. This demonstrates that the force pulling on the wall (through the fixed balance) equals the force applied through the free balance. The wall exerts an equal and opposite reaction force through the fixed balance, maintaining equilibrium.

topics.topic_name: Walking as an Application of Third Law

content.text: When we walk, we push the ground backward with our feet. According to the third law, the ground pushes our feet forward with equal force. This forward reaction force accelerates our body forward. Without the friction between our feet and ground (which provides the normal reaction), we cannot walk.

On a frictionless surface (like ice), it is difficult to walk because friction cannot provide the reaction force needed to propel us forward. On the Moon (in spacesuits), astronauts must use a different walking method because the friction is very low.

content.title: Important Point
content.text: We tend to think of the ground as 'pushing us forward,' but more accurately: we push the ground backward (action), and the ground pushes us forward (reaction). Both forces are equal and opposite. The 'effort' we feel is the muscular work done to push the ground.

topics.topic_name: Gun Recoil: Different Accelerations, Equal Forces

content.text: When a gun fires a bullet, the gunpowder explodes, pushing the bullet forward (action force on bullet). By the third law, the bullet pushes the gun backward (reaction force on gun). These forces are equal in magnitude.

However, observe the results: the bullet accelerates to a very high velocity (hundreds of m/s), while the gun recoils slowly. Why? Using F = m·a, the same force F produces different accelerations a because of different masses:
For bullet: F = m_bullet × a_bullet, so a_bullet = F / m_bullet (small mass, large acceleration)
For gun: F = m_gun × a_gun, so a_gun = F / m_gun (large mass, small acceleration)

content.title: Key Insight
content.text: Equal and opposite forces produce very different accelerations if the masses are different. This is NOT a violation of the third law — the law only requires equal forces, not equal accelerations. The accelerations depend on the respective masses through F = m·a.

topics.topic_name: Sailor and Rowing Boat: Third Law in Action

content.text: A sailor in a rowing boat wants to jump toward the shore (action force on boat, pushing it backward). By the third law, the boat pushes the sailor forward, aiding the jump. The boat moves backward in the opposite direction. If both sailor and boat have comparable mass, both move — one forward, one backward. If the boat is very massive, its backward motion is negligible.
This demonstrates that action and reaction forces are instantaneous and simultaneous — as soon as the sailor pushes the boat, the boat pushes back.

topics.topic_name: Why Action and Reaction Don't Cancel

content.text: A common misconception: 'If action and reaction are equal and opposite, why doesn't everything remain stationary?' The answer is that action and reaction act on different objects. According to Newton's second law (F = m·a), each force acts on a different object and can produce different accelerations based on the masses.

content.title: Pushing Against a Wall
content.description: You push a wall with force F (action). The wall pushes you with force F (reaction). Both forces are equal in magnitude. Yet you don't move (much), and neither does the wall. Why? Because: (1) The wall is attached to Earth, a massive system. The wall's mass is effectively infinite, so its acceleration from force F is negligible. (2) Friction between your feet and ground prevents your motion. The ground exerts a reaction force that balances your push on the wall. It's not that action and reaction cancel — it's that other forces (friction, Earth's mass) complicate the scenario.

content.title: Critical Point
content.text: Action-reaction pairs are equal and opposite, but they act on DIFFERENT objects. They can only 'cancel' if we're considering a system as a whole and using conservation of momentum. For individual objects, each feels its own force and accelerates accordingly.

Friction is a force that opposes relative motion between two surfaces in contact. Static friction (when objects are not moving) is larger and can prevent motion. Kinetic friction (when objects are moving) is smaller and acts opposite to the direction of motion.

For an object moving at constant velocity, the applied force equals the friction force (balanced forces). For an object accelerating, the applied force exceeds the friction force (unbalanced forces). This is why maintaining uniform motion in the presence of friction requires a continuous force equal to friction — not because the object 'needs' force to keep moving, but because friction opposes motion.

Newton's second law directly relates force and the rate of change of momentum: F = d(p)/dt, where p = m·v. If momentum changes (either mass changes, velocity changes, or both), force is required. If momentum remains constant (conservation of momentum), no net external force acts.

This connection is powerful: it allows us to analyze collisions, explosions, and rocket propulsion using the principle of conservation of momentum, even when the internal forces during the event are complex.

glossary.definition: A push, pull, or hit that can change the state of motion, direction, or shape of an object. It is an external agent that causes acceleration or deformation.
Two or more forces acting on an object in opposite directions with equal magnitude such that their net effect is zero. Balanced forces do not produce acceleration.
Forces acting on an object such that their net effect is non-zero. Unbalanced forces always produce acceleration in the direction of the net force.
The natural tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion or rest. All objects possess inertia; greater mass means greater inertia.
A quantitative measure of an object's inertia. It is the amount of matter in an object and remains constant regardless of location. SI unit: kilogram (kg).
The product of an object's mass and velocity (p = m·v). It is a vector quantity representing the 'quantity of motion' an object possesses. SI unit: kg·m/s.
The rate of change of velocity with respect to time. It is a vector quantity. SI unit: m/s². Acceleration can result from change in speed, direction, or both.
The SI unit of force. One newton is the force that produces an acceleration of 1 m/s² in a mass of 1 kg. 1 N = 1 kg·m/s².
The product of force and the time duration over which it acts (J = F·t). Impulse equals the change in momentum (J = Δp).
Two equal and opposite forces exerted by two objects on each other simultaneously. They act on different objects and can produce different accelerations depending on the masses.
A force that opposes relative motion between two surfaces in contact. It acts opposite to the direction of motion or intended motion.
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object. It determines the acceleration of the object according to Newton's second law (F_net = m·a).

glossary.term: First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia)
glossary.definition: An object remains in a state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled to change that state by an applied unbalanced force.
The rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of the force (F = m·a or F·t = Δp).
To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. When one object exerts a force on another, the second object exerts an equal and opposite force on the first.

summary_points: Force is a push, pull, or hit that changes an object's state of motion, direction, or shape. It can only be understood through its effects, not directly observed.
Balanced forces have zero net effect and do not change an object's motion. Unbalanced forces produce acceleration in the direction of the net force.
Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia): Objects resist changes in motion. No unbalanced force is required to maintain uniform motion, only to initiate or stop it. Inertia is measured by mass.
Newton's Second Law: F = m·a. Force equals mass times acceleration. Equivalently, force equals the rate of change of momentum (F·t = Δp or impulse-momentum form).
Momentum (p = m·v) is a measure of an object's 'quantity of motion.' It combines both mass and velocity. The change in momentum equals the impulse applied (F·t).

Newton's Third Law: Action and reaction forces are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and act on different objects. They do not cancel each other; each produces acceleration in its respective object based on its mass.

Understanding force and motion requires recognizing that forces cause acceleration, not velocity. An object can move with constant velocity without any net external force (if initially in motion or if balanced forces act).

Real-world applications of Newton's laws include vehicle braking, catching fast-moving objects, rocket propulsion, gun recoil, and understanding friction. Safety devices like airbags and seatbelts work by modifying forces and time to reduce injury.
The relationship F = m·a shows that the same force produces different accelerations in objects with different masses. This explains why heavy objects are harder to accelerate than light objects.

Impulse-momentum theorem (F·t = Δp) is fundamental to understanding collisions and interactions. It shows that the same momentum change can result from large force for short time or small force for long time, which is the principle behind safety features.
